meta.title: Concept Primer
meta.description: A primer on modern frontend web infrastructure — 50 terms defined from first principles with analogies from adjacent domains. Read once top-to-bottom, then use as reference.

blocks.content:
# Figma Make Platform PM — Concept Primer: Frontend Web Infrastructure

This primer covers 50 terms you will encounter throughout the Figma Make Platform PM curriculum. Read it top-to-bottom once — each section builds on the previous, so the order matters. After that first pass, use it as a reference document: when a curriculum module mentions "WebContainers" or "V8 isolates," return here for the definition. Where concepts connect to common backend and cloud infrastructure experience, the entries say so explicitly. Where the concept is genuinely new territory, the entries use analogies from adjacent domains (Python, AWS, agents, RAG pipelines) to build the mental model before introducing the new vocabulary. The goal is not to make you an engineer — it is to make you precise and fluent when discussing these concepts with the engineering team building Figma Make.

---

## Part 1: How Code Runs in a Browser (Fundamentals)

### 1. DOM (Document Object Model)

**What it is:** The DOM is the browser's live, in-memory representation of a web page. When a browser loads HTML, it parses the markup and builds a tree of objects — one node per element. JavaScript can then read and modify this tree, which causes the page to update visually without reloading.

**Why it exists:** HTML is a static text format. The DOM transforms that static text into a mutable data structure that code can query and manipulate at runtime. Without the DOM, you'd have to reload the entire page to change anything.

**Analogy:** Think of the DOM like a JSON object that represents your entire page. In a RAG pipeline, you query and mutate a data structure in memory; the DOM is the analogous structure for a web page. Modifying a DOM node (`document.getElementById("title").textContent = "Hello"`) is like updating a key in a Python dict — the change is live immediately.

**Where you'll see it:** Referenced in every curriculum module that discusses React, Virtual DOM, and how Figma's canvas renders to screen.

**Concrete example:** When you click "Like" on a post and the count increments instantly without a page refresh, JavaScript found the counter node in the DOM, read its current value, added 1, and wrote it back — all in milliseconds.

---

### 2. JavaScript Engine (V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore)

**What it is:** A JavaScript engine is the runtime that actually executes JavaScript code. It parses the text of your `.js` file, compiles it to machine code (using a technique called JIT — just-in-time compilation), and runs it. V8 is Google's engine (used in Chrome and Node.js). SpiderMonkey is Mozilla's (Firefox). JavaScriptCore is Apple's (Safari).

**Why it exists:** Browsers need a consistent, fast way to execute the JavaScript that web pages ship. Engines evolved from simple interpreters to sophisticated JIT compilers because JavaScript performance matters enormously for user experience.

**Analogy:** CPython is to Python as V8 is to JavaScript — it's the underlying runtime that takes your source code and actually executes it. Just as CPython compiles Python to bytecode at runtime, V8 compiles JavaScript to optimized machine code on the fly.

**Where you'll see it:** Critical in the WebContainers and sandboxing sections. V8 is also the engine powering Cloudflare Workers (V8 isolates, defined in Part 8).

**Concrete example:** When you open Chrome DevTools and type `1 + 1` in the console, V8 is the thing that evaluates that expression and returns `2`. When you run `node script.js` on your laptop, that's also V8 — the same engine, just outside the browser.

---

### 3. WebAssembly (WASM)

**What it is:** WebAssembly is a binary instruction format designed to run at near-native speed inside a browser. Code written in C, C++, Rust, or other compiled languages can be compiled to WASM and executed by the browser's JavaScript engine. It runs in the same sandbox as JavaScript but is much faster for compute-heavy tasks.

**Why it exists:** JavaScript is fast for DOM manipulation and logic, but it struggles with computationally intensive workloads — image processing, physics simulations, vector graphics rendering. WASM fills that gap by letting compiled, strongly-typed languages run in the browser without being rewritten in JavaScript.

**Analogy:** Think of WASM like a Docker image for browser computation. You compile your C++ code once to a `.wasm` binary, and any browser can run it — no native installation, no OS dependency. It's portable compiled code, similar in spirit to how a Lambda deployment package bundles a runtime with your function code.

**Where you'll see it:** Critical in the Figma context. Figma's entire rendering engine — its canvas, vector math, and rendering pipeline — is written in C++ and compiled to WASM. This is why Figma runs faster in the browser than most "native-feeling" web apps. Every time you drag a frame or resize a component, WASM is executing.

**Concrete example:** Figma renders millions of vector operations per second in your browser tab. That's only possible because the rendering code is compiled C++ running as WASM, not JavaScript. If it were pure JavaScript, it would be 10–100x slower.

---

### 4. Service Workers

**What it is:** A Service Worker is a JavaScript script that runs in the background of the browser, separate from the main page. It acts as a programmable proxy between your web app and the network — it can intercept HTTP requests, cache responses, and serve assets offline. It has no access to the DOM.

**Why it exists:** Web apps need offline capability, push notifications, and background sync — behaviors that require persistent execution decoupled from any specific browser tab. Service Workers provide that background execution context.

**Analogy:** A Service Worker is like an AWS Lambda function sitting between your frontend and your API — it intercepts every outgoing request and can respond from cache, modify the request, or pass it through. The difference is it lives inside the browser, not in a cloud datacenter.

**Where you'll see it:** WebContainers (StackBlitz's technology) use Service Workers heavily — they intercept network requests from the in-browser Node.js runtime to route them to the virtual filesystem rather than the actual internet.

**Concrete example:** When you use Google Docs offline, a Service Worker has cached your documents and is intercepting your edit requests, queuing them locally, and syncing when connectivity returns. You can close and reopen the tab — the Service Worker persists independently of the page.

---

### 5. iframe

**What it is:** An `<iframe>` (inline frame) is an HTML element that embeds a separate web page inside the current one. The embedded page is isolated — it has its own DOM, its own JavaScript context, and its own origin. The two pages can communicate only through a constrained API (`postMessage`).

**Why it exists:** iframes enable embedding third-party content (YouTube videos, Google Maps, payment forms) without giving that third party access to your page's data. They're also used to sandbox untrusted code — running it in a contained context where it can't harm the parent page.

**Analogy:** Think of an iframe like a Docker container running inside your main application process. It's isolated, has its own environment, and can only communicate through defined channels. In agent architectures, this is analogous to tool use with restricted permissions — the agent can call the tool but can't access the orchestrator's memory directly.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma's plugin system runs plugins inside sandboxed iframes. The plugin code cannot directly access Figma's internal data model; it must use the Figma Plugin API, which passes messages across the iframe boundary.

**Concrete example:** When Stripe's payment form appears embedded in a checkout page, it's inside an iframe. The merchant's page cannot read your credit card number — Stripe's iframe is isolated. The merchant's page just receives a token confirming payment success.

---

### 6. ESM (ES Modules) vs CommonJS

**What it is:** These are two different systems for organizing JavaScript code into reusable modules. CommonJS (CJS) is the older system, used in Node.js: you `require()` modules and `module.exports` to share them. ES Modules (ESM) is the newer, standardized system: you use `import` and `export` keywords, and it's natively supported in browsers.

**Why it exists:** Before modules, JavaScript had no built-in way to split code across files. CommonJS was Node.js's solution in 2009. ESM was standardized in 2015 as the official JavaScript spec, with browser support following. The ecosystem is still migrating — many packages exist in both formats.

**Analogy:** CommonJS `require()` is synchronous — like importing a Python module with `import` at the top of a script. ESM `import` is statically analyzable — the JavaScript engine knows the full dependency graph before executing any code, which enables tree shaking (defined in Part 4). Think of ESM as a pip requirements file that gets resolved before your script starts, vs CommonJS as dynamic imports that happen at runtime.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma Make kits use ESM specifically, and `esm.sh` (a CDN that converts npm packages to ESM format) is a core part of the Make kit architecture. This distinction matters when discussing why Make can run in the browser without a traditional bundler.

**Concrete example:** In a Make kit's `index.html`, you might see `<script type="module" src="main.js">`. That `type="module"` tells the browser to treat the script as ESM — enabling native browser module loading without Webpack or any build tool.

---

### 7. Same-Origin Policy / Sandbox

**What it is:** The same-origin policy is a browser security rule: JavaScript on `app.example.com` cannot read data from `api.otherdomain.com` without explicit permission. Two URLs share an "origin" only if their protocol, domain, and port all match. "Sandboxing" is the broader concept of running code in a restricted environment with limited access to system resources.

**Why it exists:** Without origin restrictions, a malicious script on any webpage could read your bank's site in another tab. The same-origin policy is the browser's primary defense against cross-site data theft.

**Analogy:** This is your IAM policy for the browser. Just as an IAM role restricts what AWS services a Lambda function can call, the same-origin policy restricts what data a web page can access. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is the mechanism for explicitly granting exceptions — like adding a trust relationship to an IAM policy.

**Where you'll see it:** Sandboxing appears throughout the curriculum when discussing WebContainers (which run Node.js in a browser sandbox), Figma plugins (which run in sandboxed iframes), and V8 isolates (which sandbox serverless functions).

**Concrete example:** If you try to `fetch()` data from a different domain's API and that API doesn't include the right CORS headers, the browser blocks the response even if the server actually returned data. The block happens client-side — the browser enforces the policy, not the server.

---

## Part 2: JavaScript Runtimes

### 8. Node.js

**What it is:** Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on V8 that runs server-side, outside the browser. It ships with a standard library for file system access, networking, HTTP servers, and process management. It's what made JavaScript viable for backend development and build tooling.

**Why it exists:** Before Node.js, JavaScript only ran in browsers. Ryan Dahl extracted V8 from Chrome in 2009 and wrapped it with OS-level APIs, enabling JavaScript to be used for servers, CLIs, and build tools — a massive ecosystem consolidation.

**Analogy:** Node.js is to JavaScript what CPython is to Python — the reference runtime that made the ecosystem viable beyond its original context. Just as Python moved from scripts to ML frameworks to web servers, JavaScript moved from browsers to backend services once Node.js existed.

**Where you'll see it:** Node.js is what npm packages are built for, what Vite runs on, and what WebContainers (StackBlitz) emulate inside the browser. Understanding "a Node.js environment" is table stakes for the Figma Make curriculum.

**Concrete example:** When you run `vite build` in a terminal, you're running a Node.js program. Vite itself is a JavaScript application — it reads your source files, bundles them, and writes output files. All of that runs in Node.js, not a browser.

---

### 9. Deno and Bun (Brief)

**What it is:** Deno and Bun are alternative JavaScript runtimes — competitors to Node.js. Deno (created by Ryan Dahl, Node's original author) emphasizes security-by-default (no file system access without explicit permission), native TypeScript support, and a URL-based module system rather than npm. Bun is newer, written in Zig, and focused on extreme performance — it's often 3–10x faster than Node.js for server startup and package installation.

**Why they exist:** Node.js accumulated architectural debt over 15 years. Deno and Bun are clean-slate reimplementations that incorporate lessons learned — better security defaults, faster performance, and native TypeScript support.

**Analogy:** Think of Deno and Bun as competitors to CPython — PyPy (faster Python) or MicroPython (constrained environments). The language (JavaScript) is the same; the runtime implementation differs in speed, security model, and ecosystem compatibility.

**Where you'll see it:** Deno is the runtime underlying Supabase Edge Functions. Bun is used by some modern build tools. You'll encounter both when the curriculum discusses serverless edge compute.

**Concrete example:** A Supabase Edge Function runs on Deno, not Node.js. This means it can't use npm packages that depend on Node.js-specific APIs — you need ESM-compatible packages instead.

---

### 10. Browser Runtime vs Server Runtime

**What it is:** The "browser runtime" is the execution environment inside a browser tab — V8 (or equivalent engine) plus browser-specific APIs like `document`, `window`, `fetch`, and `navigator`. The "server runtime" is an execution environment outside the browser — Node.js, Deno, or Bun — with access to OS-level APIs like the file system, network sockets, and environment variables, but without `document` or `window`.

**Why it exists:** The distinction matters because code written for one environment often can't run in the other. A Node.js package that uses `fs.readFileSync()` won't run in a browser — there's no file system. A browser script that references `document.querySelector()` won't run in Node.js — there's no DOM.

**Analogy:** This is analogous to the difference between Lambda (server runtime — has AWS credentials, file system access, network) and a browser-side JavaScript snippet (browser runtime — has DOM access, user input, local storage). Backend developers navigate this distinction constantly when deciding what logic belongs in a Lambda vs a frontend component.

**Where you'll see it:** WebContainers collapse this distinction — they run a Node.js server runtime inside the browser. This is architecturally significant and is why WebContainers enable fully in-browser development environments.

**Concrete example:** React's `useEffect` hook only runs in the browser runtime (it needs the DOM). That's why Next.js has Server Components (no `useEffect`, no browser APIs) and Client Components (full browser runtime access). This split is defined further in Part 3.

---

## Part 3: Frontend Frameworks

### 11. React (Component Model, JSX, Props, State)

**What it is:** React is a JavaScript library for building UIs by composing small, reusable "components." Each component is a function that takes input data ("props") and returns a description of what to render. Components manage local "state" — data that, when changed, causes the component to re-render automatically. JSX is a syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like markup inside JavaScript functions.

**Why it exists:** Before component-based frameworks, building complex UIs meant manually synchronizing application state with DOM updates — error-prone and hard to scale. React introduced a model where you declare "this is what the UI should look like given this state" and the framework handles the DOM updates.

**Analogy:** A React component is like a Lambda function handler: it takes an event (props), has access to context (state), and returns a structured response (rendered UI). The framework handles orchestration — you just define the transformation. This is the same declarative pattern as defining agent tool schemas: describe the shape, let the runtime handle execution.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits use React 18. Understanding component model, props, and state is prerequisite knowledge for discussing how Make generates code that Figma's agent writes into a live preview.

**Concrete example:** A `<Button label="Submit" onClick={handleSubmit} />` component receives `label` and `onClick` as props. When clicked, it might update state (`const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false)`), causing the component to re-render with a spinner. The developer wrote the transformation; React handled the DOM update.

---

### 12. Virtual DOM

**What it is:** The Virtual DOM (VDOM) is React's in-memory representation of the real DOM. Instead of directly updating the browser's DOM on every state change (which is slow), React first updates a lightweight JavaScript copy of the DOM tree, compares it to the previous version ("diffing"), and then applies only the minimal set of real DOM changes needed.

**Why it exists:** Direct DOM manipulation is expensive — each change can trigger browser layout recalculation, repainting, and reflow. The VDOM batches and minimizes those operations, making UI updates faster.

**Analogy:** Think of the VDOM like a write buffer in a database. Rather than writing every small change directly to disk (slow), a write buffer accumulates changes and flushes them in optimized batches. The VDOM is that buffer — it accumulates UI changes and flushes them to the real DOM efficiently.

**Where you'll see it:** VDOM is mentioned when the curriculum discusses React's architecture. Note: modern alternatives like Svelte skip the VDOM entirely, compiling components to direct DOM manipulation at build time.

**Concrete example:** If a state change affects 5 nodes in a 500-node DOM tree, React's VDOM diff identifies exactly those 5 nodes and updates only them. Without VDOM, a naive implementation might re-render the entire page — or a developer would manually write 5 specific DOM update calls, which is error-prone at scale.

---

### 13. Next.js (and How It Differs from React)

**What it is:** Next.js is a framework built on top of React that adds server-side rendering, file-based routing, API routes, image optimization, and deployment conventions. React is a library — it only handles the UI layer. Next.js is a full-stack framework that uses React for the UI but wraps it with server infrastructure.

**Why it exists:** Building a production React application requires solving many problems React doesn't address: routing, data fetching, server rendering for SEO, code splitting, and deployment. Next.js solves these with opinionated defaults, so teams don't assemble the stack themselves.

**Analogy:** React is like Boto3 — a library for a specific job (UI rendering). Next.js is like the AWS CDK — a framework that uses the library but adds infrastructure conventions, deployment pipelines, and opinionated patterns. You can use Boto3 without CDK; you can use React without Next.js. But for production, the framework adds essential scaffolding.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum distinguishes between apps built with raw React (like Figma Make kits, which are intentionally lightweight) vs production apps built with Next.js. Understanding this distinction helps you explain why Make kits don't need Next.js.

**Concrete example:** A raw React app (`create-react-app`) is a purely client-side single-page app — one HTML file, JavaScript loads, React takes over. A Next.js app can serve different pages from the server, pre-render content for SEO, and expose API endpoints — all within the same codebase.

---

### 14. Server Components vs Client Components

**What it is:** This is a Next.js distinction (introduced in Next.js 13 with React 18's Server Components feature). Server Components render on the server — they can access databases, files, and environment variables directly, but they cannot use browser APIs, state, or event handlers. Client Components render in the browser — they have access to `useState`, `useEffect`, and DOM events, but cannot directly access server resources.

**Why it exists:** Not every component needs browser interactivity. A product page's static content (title, description, images) can be rendered once on the server, sent as HTML, and never re-rendered in the browser. This improves performance and SEO. Mixing server and client components lets you put interactivity only where it's needed.

**Analogy:** This maps cleanly to agent architecture patterns. Server Components are like read-only tool calls that return data — they execute server-side and return structured output. Client Components are like the agent's interactive reasoning loop — they maintain state, respond to inputs, and operate in real time. Graduated autonomy for rendering.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum mentions this when contrasting Make kit architecture (pure client-side, no server components) against production Next.js applications. Figma Make generates code that runs client-side — understanding why helps explain the architectural choice.

**Concrete example:** A blog post page in Next.js might have a Server Component that fetches the post content from a database (no JavaScript sent to browser for this) and a Client Component for the comment form (needs `useState` for input tracking). The page ships less JavaScript to the browser than if everything were a Client Component.

---

### 15. Vue and Svelte (Brief Comparison)

**What it is:** Vue and Svelte are React alternatives — frontend frameworks for building UIs. Vue uses a "Single File Component" format (`.vue` files that contain HTML, JavaScript, and CSS together) and has a reactivity system built on JavaScript Proxies. Svelte is a compiler — it transforms component code at build time into direct, optimized DOM manipulation code, skipping the Virtual DOM entirely.

**Why they exist:** React has trade-offs: the Virtual DOM adds overhead, JSX requires a build step, and React's programming model has a learning curve. Vue is often considered more approachable. Svelte produces smaller, faster runtime code at the cost of a more complex build step.

**Analogy:** Think of React as Python's Django (opinionated, large ecosystem, battle-tested), Vue as Flask (lightweight, approachable, less opinionated), and Svelte as a compiled language like Go — it trades developer ergonomics for raw performance.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum is React-focused. Vue and Svelte appear when discussing the broader ecosystem and when competitors to Make (like Anima or other design-to-code tools) support non-React targets.

**Concrete example:** A Svelte component for a counter compiles down to three lines of vanilla DOM manipulation JavaScript. The equivalent React component ships with React's entire runtime (45KB min+gzip). For a simple static site, Svelte's output is dramatically smaller.

---

## Part 4: Build Tools & Dev Servers

### 16. npm and node_modules

**What it is:** npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager for JavaScript. It downloads packages from the npm registry (`npmjs.com`) into a local `node_modules/` directory in your project. You install packages with `npm install`, which reads `package.json` and downloads all listed dependencies.

**Why it exists:** JavaScript's ecosystem is distributed as thousands of small, composable packages. npm provides the infrastructure to find, download, version, and manage those dependencies locally.

**Analogy:** npm is pip. `node_modules/` is your Python virtual environment's `site-packages/`. The npm registry is PyPI. `npm install` is `pip install -r requirements.txt`. One key difference: `node_modules/` lives inside your project directory (not a global environment), which means it can be enormous — it's infamous for containing hundreds of megabytes of nested dependencies.

**Where you'll see it:** Every frontend project discussed in the curriculum starts with `npm install`. Understanding that npm packages must be resolved before code can run is important for grasping why WebContainers need to emulate this process in the browser.

**Concrete example:** `npm install react react-dom` downloads React (and its dependency tree) into `node_modules/`. Your source code then `import`s from these local packages — not from a URL, but from the local directory. When Vite builds your app, it resolves these local imports and bundles them.

---

### 17. package.json

**What it is:** `package.json` is the manifest file for a JavaScript project. It declares the project's name, version, scripts (commands like `build`, `dev`, `test`), and dependencies (packages needed to run) plus devDependencies (packages needed only during development, like bundlers and linters). It's the single source of truth for what a project needs to run.

**Why it exists:** Without a manifest, there's no way to declare "this project needs version 18 of React and version 4 of a specific testing library." `package.json` provides reproducible dependency declarations.

**Analogy:** `package.json` is `requirements.txt` plus a `Makefile` combined. The `dependencies` and `devDependencies` fields are your `requirements.txt`. The `scripts` field is your Makefile — running `npm run build` executes whatever command is mapped to `"build"` in that file.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits have a `package.json`. When the curriculum discusses what a Make kit "is," the `package.json` defines its dependencies (React, Vite) and the commands used to run and build it.

**Concrete example:**
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "vite",
    "build": "vite build"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "^18.0.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "vite": "^5.0.0"
  }
}
```
Running `npm run dev` starts the Vite dev server. Running `npm run build` produces a production bundle. The distinction between `dependencies` and `devDependencies` affects what gets bundled into the final output.

---

### 18. Bundler (Webpack vs esbuild vs Vite)

**What it is:** A bundler takes your many JavaScript source files (and their npm dependencies) and combines them into one or a few optimized files that browsers can load efficiently. Modern bundlers also handle transpilation (TypeScript to JavaScript), tree shaking (removing unused code), and code splitting (loading only what's needed for the current page).

**Why it exists:** Browsers historically had to make one HTTP request per file. A project with 500 source files would trigger 500 HTTP requests on page load — catastrophically slow. Bundling combines them. Even with HTTP/2 (which parallelizes requests), bundling enables optimizations like tree shaking that reduce total code size.

**Analogy:** A bundler is like packaging a Lambda deployment ZIP — you gather all the code and dependencies your function needs, compress and optimize the package, and produce a single artifact that runs in the target environment. The difference is the target is a browser instead of Lambda's execution environment.

**Three key players:**
- **Webpack:** The original, most powerful, slowest. Highly configurable, enormous ecosystem. Still common in older projects.
- **esbuild:** Written in Go, extremely fast (10–100x faster than Webpack). Limited plugin ecosystem. Often used as the underlying engine inside other tools.
- **Vite:** Uses esbuild for dependency pre-bundling and Rollup for production builds. The current industry standard. Defined in depth below.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits use Vite. The curriculum references bundlers when explaining the build step that produces deployable artifacts.

**Concrete example:** Your project has 200 `import` statements across 50 files. Running `vite build` resolves all imports, eliminates dead code, minifies the result, and outputs a single `bundle.js` file (perhaps 120KB instead of the 2MB of raw source). That one file is what ships to the browser.

---

### 19. Dev Server (What It Is, Why It Exists)

**What it is:** A dev server is a local HTTP server that serves your source files during development — with extras like Hot Module Replacement, error overlays, and proxy support. It's not your production server; it's a development tool that makes the edit-refresh cycle fast and ergonomic.

**Why it exists:** You can't just open a `.js` file in a browser during development — browsers won't load ES Modules from the local file system due to same-origin restrictions. The dev server solves this by serving files over `http://localhost`, and it adds features (live reloading, error display) that make development faster.

**Analogy:** A dev server is to frontend development what `uvicorn --reload app:main` is to FastAPI development. Both watch for file changes and reload automatically. Both serve over localhost. Both are development-only — in production, you use a proper server (Nginx, Gunicorn) instead.

**Where you'll see it:** Every frontend workflow in the curriculum starts with `npm run dev`, which starts the dev server. WebContainers run a dev server inside the browser, enabling in-browser editing with the same live-reload experience you'd have locally.

**Concrete example:** You run `npm run dev` in a terminal. Vite starts a dev server at `http://localhost:5173`. You open that URL in Chrome. You edit `Button.tsx` in VSCode. Within 100ms, the browser updates — just the `Button` component changes, not the whole page. That's the dev server + HMR working together.

---

### 20. Vite (The Current Standard)

**What it is:** Vite is a frontend build tool and dev server that has become the industry default for React, Vue, and Svelte projects. In development, Vite serves source files directly as ES Modules — no bundling — using native browser module loading. This makes cold starts instant. For production builds, Vite uses Rollup to produce optimized bundles. Hot Module Replacement (defined next) is built in.

**Why it exists:** Webpack-era dev servers bundled your entire application before serving it — as apps grew, this meant waiting 30–60 seconds for the dev server to start. Vite's insight: modern browsers support ES Modules natively. Serve the source files directly; let the browser resolve imports. Start time drops to under 1 second regardless of app size.

**Analogy:** Vite is to frontend what `uvicorn --reload` is to FastAPI — a fast, opinionated development server with automatic reloading. But Vite adds something FastAPI's dev server doesn't: in production mode, it also acts as your build pipeline (transpiling, tree shaking, minifying). One tool for both dev and prod.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits use Vite. `vite.config.ts` is the project's build configuration file. `npm run dev` starts Vite's dev server; `npm run build` produces the production bundle.

**Concrete example:** Cold start comparison: A medium-sized app on Webpack dev server takes 45 seconds to start. The same app on Vite starts in 400ms. This isn't a configuration win — it's architectural. Vite doesn't pre-bundle in dev mode; it transforms files on demand as the browser requests them.

---

### 21. Hot Module Replacement (HMR)

**What it is:** HMR is a dev server feature that updates only the changed module in a running application without refreshing the full page. When you edit a component, HMR sends the new version to the browser, swaps it in, and the page updates — preserving application state (like form inputs or scroll position) that a full refresh would destroy.

**Why it exists:** Full page refreshes during development are disruptive — you lose state, wait for re-initialization, and navigate back to the section you were testing. HMR makes the feedback loop nearly continuous.

**Analogy:** HMR is like hot-swapping a Lambda function alias to point to a new version — without downtime. The function (component) updates; active connections (browser state) aren't interrupted. In a RAG pipeline analogy: HMR is like updating a retrieval prompt template without restarting the entire inference pipeline.

**Where you'll see it:** HMR is a key selling point of Vite. The curriculum mentions it when discussing the developer experience of working with Make kits, and again when explaining how WebContainers provide browser-native development environments with the same experience.

**Concrete example:** You're testing a multi-step form. You're on Step 3 with data already entered. You spot a CSS typo in the Step 3 heading. You fix it in your editor. HMR swaps in the new component — the heading text updates, but you're still on Step 3 with your data intact. Without HMR, the page reloads and you start from Step 1.

---

### 22. Transpilation (TypeScript → JS, JSX → JS)

**What it is:** Transpilation is source-to-source compilation — transforming code from one language (or language variant) to another. The two most common cases in frontend development: TypeScript (typed JavaScript) transpiles to plain JavaScript so browsers can run it, and JSX (React's HTML-in-JavaScript syntax) transpiles to standard JavaScript function calls.

**Why it exists:** Browsers only understand standard JavaScript. TypeScript and JSX are developer conveniences — they add type safety and syntactic sugar that browsers don't natively support. Transpilation converts them to browser-executable JavaScript at build time.

**Analogy:** Transpilation is like compiling a CloudFormation YAML template from CDK TypeScript — you write in a higher-level syntax that's easier for developers, and the toolchain converts it to what the target environment actually needs. The CDK TypeScript is never deployed; the CloudFormation YAML is. JSX is never deployed; the transpiled JavaScript is.

**Where you'll see it:** Vite handles transpilation automatically. TypeScript errors appear in your editor and build output. The curriculum references transpilation when explaining what happens during `vite build` and why raw JSX files can't be served directly to browsers.

**Concrete example:** You write `const greeting = <h1>Hello</h1>` in JSX. After transpilation, this becomes `const greeting = React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello")` — a plain JavaScript function call the browser understands. The JSX is never executed; it's an authoring convenience.

---

### 23. Tree Shaking and Minification

**What it is:** Tree shaking is a bundler optimization that removes unused code. If you `import { Button } from 'ui-library'` but the library exports 50 components, tree shaking eliminates the 49 you didn't use from the final bundle. Minification removes whitespace, shortens variable names, and compresses code — purely a size optimization without changing behavior.

**Why they exist:** JavaScript bundle size directly affects page load time, especially on mobile. Shipping 500KB of JavaScript to render a simple page is wasteful. Tree shaking reduces it to what's actually needed; minification compresses what remains.

**Analogy:** Tree shaking is like Lambda layer optimization — only including the Python packages your function actually calls, not the entire layer. Minification is like gzip compression on a response body — same content, smaller payload.

**Where you'll see it:** Both happen automatically during `vite build`. The curriculum references them when explaining why production bundles are dramatically smaller than development source. Tree shaking only works with ESM imports (not CommonJS `require()`) — this is a key reason ESM adoption matters.

**Concrete example:** You install lodash (a 70KB utility library) and use only its `debounce` function. With CommonJS, the entire 70KB ships. With ESM + tree shaking, only the `debounce` code (2KB) is included. The 97% reduction compounds across every dependency in a large application.

---

## Part 5: Styling

### 24. Tailwind CSS (Utility-First CSS)

**What it is:** Tailwind is a CSS framework that provides hundreds of single-purpose utility classes (`flex`, `p-4`, `text-blue-500`, `rounded-lg`) instead of pre-built components. Rather than writing a `.card { padding: 16px; border-radius: 8px; }` CSS rule, you apply `p-4 rounded-lg` directly to your HTML element. Tailwind's build step scans your code and outputs only the CSS classes you actually used.

**Why it exists:** Traditional CSS approaches lead to bloated stylesheets, naming conflicts, and difficult-to-maintain codebases as projects scale. Tailwind trades semantic CSS naming (`.card`) for co-located styling (the classes are right on the element) — which is faster to write, easier to reason about locally, and produces smaller CSS files via tree shaking.

**Analogy:** Tailwind is like inline Lambda environment variables instead of a separate config file — you put the configuration (styling) right where you use it (the component), rather than in a separate file you have to cross-reference. It's "configuration as code" applied to CSS.

**Where you'll see it:** Most modern React projects use Tailwind, including production-grade Figma Make kit implementations. The curriculum references it when discussing design token implementation and the connection between Figma design decisions and code output.

**Concrete example:** A button styled with Tailwind: `<button className="bg-blue-500 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded hover:bg-blue-600">`. No separate CSS file needed. The classes are self-documenting — blue background, white text, horizontal/vertical padding, rounded corners, darker blue on hover.

---

### 25. Design Tokens

**What it is:** Design tokens are named variables that store design decisions — colors, font sizes, spacing values, border radii. Rather than hardcoding `#1A73E8` everywhere, you define a token `color.primary.500 = #1A73E8` and reference the token name throughout your codebase. Changing the token propagates the change everywhere it's used.

**Why they exist:** Design and engineering teams need a shared language for design decisions. When a designer says "the primary action color changed from blue to teal," tokens let that change propagate through both the design tool (Figma) and the codebase automatically — rather than hunting for every hardcoded value.

**Analogy:** Design tokens are environment variables for visual design. Just as `DATABASE_URL` in a `.env` file lets you change the database connection without touching application code, `color.primary.500` lets you change the brand color without touching component code. They're the "configuration, not code" principle applied to design systems.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma stores design decisions as Figma Variables, which map directly to design tokens. The Make curriculum discusses how Figma Variables export as tokens that can be consumed by Tailwind configuration or CSS variables in code, creating a single source of truth between design and implementation.

**Concrete example:** Figma has a variable `color/brand/primary = #3B82F6`. This exports as a CSS variable `--color-brand-primary: #3B82F6` and maps to a Tailwind config entry. Every component using `text-brand-primary` or `var(--color-brand-primary)` automatically reflects a brand color change when the Figma variable is updated and tokens are re-exported.

---

## Part 6: Networking & Hosting

### 26. HTTP Request/Response Cycle

**What it is:** HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the protocol for data exchange on the web. Every interaction follows a request/response pattern: a client (browser) sends a request to a server, specifying a method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), a URL, headers (metadata), and optionally a body. The server processes the request and returns a response with a status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error), headers, and a body.

**Why it exists:** HTTP provides a universal, stateless protocol for structured data exchange. Its simplicity and ubiquity made it the foundation of the web and, by extension, REST APIs.

**Analogy:** If you've worked with backend APIs — Bedrock, REST services, Lambda function URLs — this is the same HTTP. The browser's `fetch()` API works identically to Python's `requests` library. The difference is the client: browser vs server process.

**Where you'll see it:** HTTP is foundational to every networking concept that follows: CDNs, DNS, SSL/TLS, serverless functions. Understanding the request/response cycle is prerequisite to understanding how edge functions intercept and respond to requests.

**Concrete example:** When your browser navigates to `https://figma.com`, it sends: `GET / HTTP/1.1 Host: figma.com`. Figma's servers respond: `HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html [body: HTML]`. Your browser parses the HTML, discovers CSS and JavaScript references, and sends additional GET requests for each asset.

---

### 27. CDN (Content Delivery Network) and Edge

**What it is:** A CDN is a globally distributed network of servers that cache and serve static assets (JavaScript, CSS, images, fonts) from locations geographically close to end users. Instead of all requests going to one origin server in one data center, a CDN intercepts requests and serves them from the nearest "edge node" — often within milliseconds of the user.

**Why it exists:** Network latency is physical — data can only travel so fast. A user in Tokyo fetching a file from a U.S.-only server experiences 150–200ms of round-trip latency just from physics. A CDN node in Tokyo can serve the same file in under 5ms.

**Analogy:** A CDN is like ElastiCache for your static files. Your origin server (like your database) holds the canonical copy. The CDN caches copies at the edge (like Redis cache nodes), serving the cached version until it expires or the origin invalidates it. The "hit" avoids the round trip to origin.

**Where you'll see it:** Every deployment platform in Part 7 (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare) is built on top of CDN infrastructure. "Edge" is used loosely to mean "geographically distributed compute close to users" — it extends the CDN concept from caching to execution.

**Concrete example:** Cloudflare operates ~300 edge locations globally. When a user in Singapore requests your Vercel-deployed site, Cloudflare's Singapore node serves cached static files without the request ever reaching your origin server in Virginia. Latency: 4ms instead of 180ms.

---

### 28. DNS (Domain Name System)

**What it is:** DNS is the internet's phone book — it translates human-readable domain names (`figma.com`) to IP addresses (`104.18.0.0`) that routers use to route traffic. When you type a URL, your browser first queries a DNS resolver, gets the IP address, then sends the HTTP request to that IP.

**Why it exists:** IP addresses are 32-bit numbers (or 128-bit for IPv6) — not memorable. DNS provides a hierarchical naming system that maps memorable names to network addresses, and allows those addresses to change without users updating anything.

**Analogy:** DNS is like a service registry or service discovery in a microservices architecture. Instead of hardcoding service IPs, services register names and look them up dynamically. AWS Route 53 is a DNS service with the same underlying logic. DNS for web domains works the same way.

**Where you'll see it:** Deployment workflows involve pointing DNS records to deployment platform IPs or CDN addresses. When you deploy to Vercel and add a custom domain, you update a DNS record to point to Vercel's infrastructure.

**Concrete example:** `dig figma.com` returns Figma's IP address. When Figma migrated hosting providers, they updated their DNS records. Users continued using `figma.com` without any change — DNS handled the remapping transparently within the TTL (time-to-live) of the old record.

---

### 29. SSL/TLS Certificates

**What it is:** SSL/TLS is the encryption protocol that makes HTTPS work. A TLS certificate is a cryptographic document that proves a server is who it claims to be (authentication) and enables encrypting the connection (confidentiality). When your browser shows a padlock icon, it means the connection is encrypted with TLS.

**Why it exists:** Without TLS, HTTP traffic is transmitted in plaintext — anyone on the network path between browser and server can read it. TLS encrypts the channel. Certificates also prevent impersonation — they prove the server is actually `figma.com` and not an attacker pretending to be Figma.

**Analogy:** TLS certificates are like AWS IAM certificates combined with KMS encryption — identity verification plus data encryption. ACM (AWS Certificate Manager) provisions TLS certificates for AWS services; Let's Encrypt and Cloudflare do the equivalent for web domains, and most deployment platforms provision them automatically.

**Where you'll see it:** Every modern deployment platform (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages) automatically provisions TLS certificates for custom domains via Let's Encrypt. This is typically invisible in modern deployments — it "just works." You encounter it explicitly only when certificates expire or misconfigure.

**Concrete example:** Vercel automatically provisions a Let's Encrypt certificate when you add `yourdomain.com` to a project. The certificate is renewed every 90 days automatically. You access your site via `https://yourdomain.com` without any manual certificate configuration.

---

### 30. Origin Server vs Edge

**What it is:** The "origin server" is your canonical server — the one that holds the authoritative copy of your data and runs your application logic. "Edge" refers to servers distributed geographically, closer to users, that sit between users and the origin. Static assets are cached at the edge. Edge functions (compute) execute at the edge without a round trip to origin.

**Why it exists:** The origin server is a single point of latency and failure. Edge servers distribute both caching and compute globally, reducing latency for users worldwide and reducing load on the origin.

**Analogy:** The origin server is like your primary RDS instance — the authoritative data store. Edge nodes are like read replicas distributed globally. Requests that can be served from a read replica (cached content, edge functions) never hit the primary. Requests requiring writes go to origin.

**Where you'll see it:** Vercel, Cloudflare, and Netlify all have edge networks. "Edge functions" (Part 7) are serverless functions deployed to edge nodes rather than a single origin region. Figma Make's live previews run at the edge — which matters for latency.

**Concrete example:** Your Next.js app has a `/about` page that's static (same content for all users). Vercel caches it on CDN edge nodes worldwide. Your `/dashboard` page is dynamic (different for each user). Each request hits the origin server. The CDN cache handles the first case; the origin handles the second.

---

## Part 7: Deployment Platforms

### 31. Vercel vs Netlify vs Cloudflare Pages

**What they are:** These are frontend deployment platforms — they take your built frontend code and serve it globally. All three provide: git-based deployments (push to GitHub → auto-deploy), global CDN hosting for static assets, serverless function support, SSL certificates, and preview deployments for pull requests.

**Key differences:**
- **Vercel:** Created by the Next.js team. Best-in-class Next.js support (Server Components, edge middleware). Premium pricing for teams. Optimized for full-stack JS apps.
- **Netlify:** Broader ecosystem support, stronger form handling and identity features, slightly more DevOps-friendly. Less tight Next.js integration.
- **Cloudflare Pages:** Built on Cloudflare's global network (300+ PoPs). Free tier is extremely generous. Integrates with Cloudflare Workers for edge compute. Fastest cold starts due to V8 isolate architecture.

**Analogy:** These are "Heroku for frontend" — managed hosting platforms that abstract away server configuration. They're to frontend deployment what AWS Elastic Beanstalk is to backend deployment: you provide code, they handle infrastructure. The difference from raw AWS is convention-over-configuration and zero-DevOps deployment.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum discusses these when explaining how Make-generated apps get deployed. Figma likely uses one of these platforms for Make preview environments.

**Concrete example:** You push a commit to your GitHub repo's `main` branch. Vercel detects the push, runs `npm run build`, deploys the output to its global CDN, and sends you a Slack notification with the live URL — in under 30 seconds, with no server configuration.

---

### 32. Serverless Functions / Lambda (Relate to What He Knows)

**What it is:** Serverless functions are single-purpose backend functions that run on demand without requiring you to provision or manage a server. You write a function handler, deploy it, and the platform handles scaling, execution, and billing (per invocation). AWS Lambda is the canonical implementation; Vercel Functions, Netlify Functions, and similar are managed alternatives.

**Why it exists:** Running a server 24/7 for infrequent backend work is wasteful. Serverless functions scale to zero when idle, scale to thousands of instances under load, and bill per millisecond of execution. They're ideal for backend logic that doesn't require persistent state.

**Analogy:** AWS Lambda is the canonical reference implementation. Vercel Functions, Netlify Functions, and Supabase Edge Functions are the same concept with different runtimes and deployment conventions. The key differences: Vercel Functions run in Node.js on AWS infrastructure (same as Lambda, just abstracted). Supabase Edge Functions run on Deno. Cloudflare Workers run on V8 isolates (lighter than Lambda's Firecracker VMs, with different trade-offs defined in Part 8).

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum references serverless functions when discussing how Make-generated apps call backend APIs, and how authentication, data persistence, and AI inference are handled without requiring a dedicated server.

**Concrete example:** A Make-generated app has a "Generate text" button. Clicking it calls a Vercel Function (`/api/generate`) which calls OpenAI's API with the user's prompt and returns the result. The Vercel Function runs for ~2 seconds, then terminates. No server is running between requests.

---

### 33. Edge Functions

**What it is:** Edge functions are serverless functions that execute at CDN edge nodes — geographically close to the user — rather than in a single origin data center. They're designed for low-latency, simple request transformations: authentication checks, A/B testing, redirects, personalization headers. They have more constraints than regular serverless functions: smaller memory limits, no Node.js APIs (typically), faster cold starts.

**Why they exist:** Running authentication middleware in a Virginia data center for a user in Tokyo adds 200ms of latency. Edge functions run that same middleware in a Tokyo edge node, adding under 5ms. For request-level logic that should happen before serving content, edge is dramatically faster.

**Analogy:** Edge functions are like Lambda@Edge — the AWS product that runs Lambda functions at CloudFront edge locations. Vercel Edge Middleware and Cloudflare Workers are the same concept with different runtime implementations. The trade-off is the same: lower latency but more constrained execution environment.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma likely uses edge functions for authentication checks on Make preview URLs. Cloudflare Workers (the most popular edge function implementation) power many developer tool backends, including, notably, the backend infrastructure for several AI coding tools.

**Concrete example:** A Vercel Edge Middleware function runs before every request to your app. It checks whether the user has a valid session cookie. If not, it redirects to `/login` — this happens at the edge node closest to the user, in milliseconds, before the request ever reaches your origin server or database.

---

### 34. Static Hosting vs SSR (Server-Side Rendering)

**What it is:** Static hosting serves pre-built HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files that are identical for every user. The server does no computation per request — it just serves files. SSR (Server-Side Rendering) generates HTML on the server for each request, potentially customizing content per user. SSR requires a running server; static hosting only requires file storage.

**Why the distinction exists:** Static sites are cheaper, faster, and more scalable (CDNs can cache them globally). But some content must be dynamic — a logged-in user's dashboard can't be a static file. SSR enables dynamic content while still sending complete HTML to the browser (better for SEO than client-side rendering).

**Analogy:** Static hosting is like serving a pre-rendered response from an S3 bucket with CloudFront in front — no compute per request. SSR is like a Lambda function that generates a response dynamically per request. The S3/CloudFront approach is cheaper at scale but can't personalize content.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits are statically hosted — the output of `vite build` is static files. Production apps with user accounts typically use SSR (Next.js). The curriculum discusses this when explaining the architectural choices in Make's preview environment.

**Concrete example:** A marketing site (same content for all visitors) is ideal for static hosting — build once, serve from CDN everywhere, near-zero cost. An e-commerce product page (inventory levels, personalized recommendations) benefits from SSR — the server fetches current data and renders it into HTML before sending.

---

## Part 8: Execution Environments

### 35. Docker Containers

**What it is:** Docker is a platform for packaging applications with their dependencies into standardized, portable units called "containers." A container bundles the application code, runtime, libraries, and OS dependencies into an image that runs identically on any host that has Docker installed. Containers share the host OS kernel but are isolated from each other.

**Why it exists:** "It works on my machine" is the canonical developer problem. Docker solves it by making the execution environment reproducible — the container is the same whether running on a developer's laptop, CI/CD pipeline, or production server.

**Analogy:** Docker containers are the unit of deployment for most AWS services — Lambda packages (`.zip` or container image), ECS tasks, SageMaker endpoints. The concept is the same everywhere: package application + dependencies into a portable, isolated unit.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum uses Docker as a baseline when explaining more specialized execution environments (Firecracker microVMs, V8 isolates, WebContainers). Understanding Docker's isolation model (namespace + cgroup isolation at the OS level) helps contrast it with lighter alternatives.

**Concrete example:** A Python ML model is packaged as a Docker image with Python 3.11, required libraries, and the model weights. That image runs identically on a developer's MacBook, the CI pipeline, and a SageMaker endpoint — the environment is self-contained.

---

### 36. Virtual Machines (VMs)

**What it is:** A Virtual Machine (VM) emulates an entire computer — its own OS kernel, drivers, and hardware abstraction — on top of physical hardware. A hypervisor (like VMware or KVM) manages multiple VMs on one physical host, each believing it's running on dedicated hardware. VMs are fully isolated: a crash in one VM doesn't affect others.

**Why they exist:** VMs enable multiple tenants to share physical hardware with strong isolation guarantees. Before containers, VMs were the standard unit of cloud compute (EC2 instances are VMs). They trade resource efficiency (each VM runs its own OS) for stronger isolation.

**Analogy:** EC2 instances are VMs. When you launch an `m5.large`, AWS creates a VM on physical hardware — you get an isolated OS environment with dedicated vCPUs and memory. That's what a VM is: an isolated operating system environment sharing underlying physical resources.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum uses VMs as context for Firecracker (which makes VM startup dramatically faster) and for contrasting the isolation models of different serverless execution environments.

**Concrete example:** Before Firecracker, AWS Lambda spun up full VMs for each function execution. A VM takes 1–5 seconds to start. This made Lambda "cold starts" slow. Firecracker solved this by making VM startup under 125ms, which is how Lambda achieves fast cold starts today.

---

### 37. Firecracker microVMs

**What it is:** Firecracker is an open-source virtual machine monitor (VMM) built by AWS, designed specifically for serverless workloads. It creates minimalist VMs ("microVMs") that boot in under 125ms, use significantly less memory than full VMs, and maintain the strong isolation guarantees of traditional VMs. AWS Lambda and AWS Fargate run on Firecracker.

**Why it exists:** Serverless computing requires rapid, multi-tenant execution of arbitrary code with strong isolation. Traditional VMs are too slow to start and too resource-heavy. Docker containers are fast but share the kernel (less isolation). Firecracker finds the middle ground: VM-level isolation with container-like speed and density.

**Analogy:** Every AWS Lambda invocation runs in a Firecracker microVM. The "cold start" penalty teams optimize for (keeping functions warm, right-sizing memory) exists because of Firecracker's 125ms+ initialization time. It's fast for a VM; it's still slower than a container or V8 isolate.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum contrasts Firecracker (Lambda's isolation model) with V8 isolates (Cloudflare Workers' isolation model) when discussing the trade-offs between serverless execution environments.

**Concrete example:** A Lambda function cold start involves: Firecracker creating a microVM (~125ms), the Node.js runtime initializing inside it (~50–200ms), and your function's initialization code running. Total: 200–500ms. Subsequent invocations reuse the warm microVM: ~1ms. This is why Lambda cold starts exist and why you care about warm instances.

---

### 38. V8 Isolates (Cloudflare Workers)

**What it is:** V8 isolates are lightweight execution contexts within the V8 JavaScript engine. They're how browsers isolate different browser tabs from each other — each tab runs in its own V8 isolate, with no shared memory or state. Cloudflare Workers repurposes this mechanism for serverless compute: each Worker function runs in its own V8 isolate, with near-zero startup time.

**Why they exist:** V8 isolates are dramatically lighter than VMs or containers. A V8 isolate starts in under 1ms — compared to Firecracker's 125ms. This enables "cold starts" so fast they're effectively imperceptible, enabling use cases that Lambda's cold start latency would prohibit.

**Analogy:** Lambda (Firecracker microVMs) is the well-known baseline. V8 isolates are the lighter, faster alternative — similar isolation goal, radically different implementation. The trade-off: V8 isolates only run JavaScript/WebAssembly (no arbitrary runtimes), have stricter memory limits, and can't use Node.js APIs. Cloudflare Workers are to Lambda as a WebAssembly module is to a Docker container — same concept, more constrained, much lighter.

**Where you'll see it:** Cloudflare Workers are increasingly used for edge compute (CDN-level functions), AI inference proxies, and developer tool backends. The curriculum contrasts V8 isolates and Firecracker when discussing why different serverless platforms have different performance characteristics.

**Concrete example:** A Cloudflare Worker responding to an API request has effectively zero cold start — sub-millisecond isolate initialization. The same function on Lambda would have a 200–500ms cold start if unwarmed. For globally distributed, latency-sensitive API endpoints, Workers' architecture is meaningfully faster.

---

### 39. WebContainers (StackBlitz's Tech)

**What it is:** WebContainers is a technology built by StackBlitz that runs a Node.js environment directly inside a browser tab — no server required. It implements the Node.js runtime, file system, and process model using browser APIs (Service Workers, SharedArrayBuffer, etc.). When you use StackBlitz or Bolt.new, your entire dev environment — including `npm install`, `vite dev`, and the live preview — runs in the browser.

**Why it exists:** Developer environments traditionally require a server (local machine or cloud VM). Provisioning, configuration, and maintenance are friction. WebContainers eliminate the server: the browser *is* the server. This enables zero-setup development environments, in-browser AI coding tools (Bolt.new), and Figma's potential in-browser Make environment.

**Analogy:** WebContainers collapse the server runtime into the browser runtime (a distinction defined in Part 2). Imagine running CPython, an entire virtual environment, and a FastAPI server inside a browser tab — that's the analogous feat. Service Workers intercept network requests that Node.js makes, routing them to the in-browser virtual file system rather than the actual network.

**Where you'll see it:** WebContainers are central to the Figma Make curriculum. Bolt.new (which Figma's Make environment may reference or resemble architecturally) runs on WebContainers. The ability to run `npm install` and `vite dev` inside a browser tab is what enables fully in-browser, agent-driven development environments.

**Concrete example:** On bolt.new, you type "build me a React todo app." Claude or GPT-4 generates the files. Those files are written to WebContainers' virtual file system. `npm install` runs in the browser (installing to an in-memory node_modules). `vite dev` starts, serving the app to a browser iframe. You see the running app — all without any server, cloud VM, or local install.

---

### 40. Sandboxing (What It Means in Different Contexts)

**What it is:** Sandboxing is the practice of running code in a restricted execution environment with limited access to system resources, other processes, and sensitive data. The goal is containment — if sandboxed code behaves maliciously or crashes, the damage is bounded. "Sandbox" is used across different layers of the stack with different implementations.

**Why it exists:** Any system that runs untrusted code (user-uploaded code, third-party plugins, AI-generated code) needs sandboxing. The threat model determines the sandbox strength required.

**Sandboxing in different contexts:**
- **Browser tabs:** V8 isolates sandbox each tab from others
- **iframes:** Same-origin policy sandboxes embedded content from the parent
- **Figma plugins:** iframes sandbox plugin code from Figma's internals
- **WebContainers:** The browser OS sandbox contains the entire Node.js environment
- **Lambda / Firecracker:** microVMs sandbox each function invocation
- **Cloudflare Workers:** V8 isolates sandbox each worker

**Analogy:** Sandboxing follows the same principle as graduated autonomy in agent architectures. The level of sandbox strength matches the level of trust — fully trusted internal code runs with full access; untrusted external code runs with constrained access. The implementation (VM, container, isolate, iframe) is the mechanism; the principle is the same as permission boundaries in agent tool systems.

**Where you'll see it:** Sandboxing is the unifying concept across Part 8 and the Figma plugin system. When Figma's agents generate and execute code in Make, sandboxing is what prevents that code from accessing sensitive user data or breaking Figma's application.

**Concrete example:** A Figma plugin runs in an iframe sandbox. It can call `figma.createRectangle()` (through the approved Plugin API) but cannot read your file's authentication tokens or make arbitrary network requests to external servers. The iframe boundary enforces these restrictions at the browser level.

---

## Part 9: Backend-as-a-Service

### 41. Supabase (What It Actually Is)

**What it is:** Supabase is an open-source Backend-as-a-Service built on PostgreSQL. It provides a hosted Postgres database, auto-generated REST and GraphQL APIs, real-time subscriptions (WebSockets that push database changes to clients), authentication (email, OAuth, magic links), file storage, and edge functions — all accessible via a JavaScript SDK or direct API calls. It's the open-source alternative to Google's Firebase.

**Why it exists:** Frontend developers building full-stack apps need a backend — database, auth, storage — but provisioning and configuring raw AWS (RDS, Cognito, S3, Lambda) requires substantial DevOps knowledge. Supabase packages these primitives with sensible defaults and a JavaScript-first developer experience.

**Analogy:** Supabase is "AWS in a box for teams that don't want to configure raw AWS." The components map directly: Supabase Database = RDS Postgres, Supabase Auth = Cognito, Supabase Storage = S3, Supabase Edge Functions = Lambda (running on Deno instead of Node.js). The difference is that Supabase configures the integration between these components and exposes them through a single SDK. Wiring up the equivalent stack on raw AWS requires days of IAM roles, VPC configs, and API Gateway setup. Supabase does it in minutes.

**Where you'll see it:** Supabase is the default database/auth backend for AI-generated apps (Bolt.new defaults to Supabase). The curriculum references it when discussing how Make-generated apps persist data and handle authentication without requiring developers to provision backend infrastructure.

**Concrete example:** A Bolt.new-generated app needs user authentication and a database for todo items. With three lines of Supabase configuration, it has: a hosted Postgres table for todos, row-level security (only see your own todos), OAuth login with Google, and real-time updates (new todos appear instantly across browser tabs). No server code written.

---

### 42. Auth.js / Clerk / Authentication Providers (Brief)

**What it is:** Authentication providers handle the complex, security-critical problem of verifying user identity. Rather than building auth from scratch (which has many failure modes), developers use specialized services. Auth.js (formerly NextAuth) is an open-source library for Next.js that integrates with OAuth providers. Clerk is a hosted service providing pre-built auth UI components, session management, and user management. Supabase Auth (above) is another option.

**Why they exist:** Authentication is notoriously hard to implement securely — session management, token rotation, OAuth flows, password hashing, and MFA have many subtle failure modes. Specialized providers implement auth correctly once; developers integrate rather than implement.

**Analogy:** Auth providers are like Cognito — managed identity infrastructure to integrate with rather than build. The difference is developer experience: Cognito is powerful but complex to configure. Clerk is opinionated and designed for rapid integration — drop in a component, auth works. Clerk is to Cognito as Supabase is to raw AWS.

**Where you'll see it:** AI-generated apps (from Bolt.new, v0, or Figma Make) that need auth typically reach for Clerk or Supabase Auth. The curriculum mentions them as the default authentication layer for Make-generated production apps.

**Concrete example:** Adding Clerk to a Next.js app: install the package, wrap your app in `<ClerkProvider>`, add `<SignInButton>` to your header. Users can now sign in with Google or GitHub. Session management, token refresh, and user profile are handled by Clerk's infrastructure. Implementation time: 15 minutes instead of weeks.

---

## Part 10: The AI Coding Tools Landscape

### 43. Code Assistants (Copilot, Cursor)

**What it is:** Code assistants are AI tools integrated into the developer's code editor that provide real-time suggestions — autocomplete, function generation, inline chat, and code explanation — while the developer writes code. GitHub Copilot is the original (built on Codex, now GPT-4). Cursor is an AI-first code editor (a fork of VSCode) with deeper integration — multi-file context, agent mode, and codebase-level understanding.

**Why they exist:** Developers spend significant time on repetitive code patterns, boilerplate, and looking up API syntax. Code assistants reduce this friction by predicting what the developer wants to write next, based on the current file and project context.

**Analogy:** Claude Code is the most sophisticated implementation of this category — codebase-level context, multi-file edits, command execution. Copilot is the simpler version: inline suggestions in a standard editor. Cursor is intermediate — an editor built around AI integration with deep codebase context.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum uses this category to contrast with Code Generators and Coding Agents, positioning Figma Make as an agent-level tool (not just a code assistant) that operates from design intent rather than developer prompts.

**Concrete example:** In Cursor, you open a file and type a comment: `// fetch user profile and display it`. Cursor generates 20 lines of React component code that matches your project's patterns and imports. You review, accept, and move on. The developer remains in control; the AI accelerates repetitive work.

---

### 44. Code Generators (v0, Lovable, Bolt)

**What it is:** Code generators take a natural language description (or a design file) and produce a complete, runnable application or component — not just a suggestion, but a working implementation. v0 (Vercel) generates React components from prompts. Lovable generates full-stack web apps. Bolt.new (StackBlitz) generates complete apps that run immediately in the browser via WebContainers.

**Why they exist:** Code generators target non-developers and developers who want to skip early prototyping. They produce starting points — not necessarily production-ready code — that can be iterated on. The gap they fill is "from idea to running prototype" without requiring deep programming knowledge.

**Analogy:** Code generators are like CloudFormation template generators — you describe the infrastructure you want, they produce the YAML, you review and deploy. The difference is the output is application code rather than infrastructure config. The PM defines the target; the generator scaffolds the first version.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma Make is positioned at this level or above. The curriculum distinguishes Make from v0 by its integration with design — Make starts from a Figma canvas (design intent), not a text prompt. This makes Make's output more faithful to the intended design than prompt-to-code generators.

**Concrete example:** You type "build a dashboard with a sidebar, user stats, and a data table" into Bolt.new. It generates a React app with those components, runs `npm install` in the browser via WebContainers, starts a Vite dev server, and shows you the running app in a preview pane — in under 60 seconds.

---

### 45. Coding Agents (Claude Code, Replit Agent, Devin)

**What it is:** Coding agents are autonomous AI systems that can plan, execute, debug, and iterate on software development tasks with minimal human intervention. They use tools (file read/write, terminal execution, web search, browser control) to complete multi-step engineering tasks. Claude Code, Replit Agent, and Devin (Cognition) are implementations. Unlike code assistants (which suggest), agents act.

**Why they exist:** Code assistants and generators still require developers to orchestrate — accept suggestions, wire up components, debug integration issues. Agents take the goal and complete it end-to-end, handling intermediate failures themselves. They represent the graduation from "AI that helps developers code" to "AI that codes."

**Analogy:** Coding agents apply the same architecture used in AI agent systems with tool use and graduated autonomy to software development. The agent has tools (file system, terminal, browser), a task, and a loop: plan → act → observe result → re-plan. Claude Code is the production reference implementation of this model.

**Where you'll see it:** Figma Make's AI agents are at this level — they receive design intent from the Figma canvas and autonomously generate, run, and iterate on code until the preview matches the design. Understanding agentic architecture (tool use, feedback loops, error recovery) is a strong point of entry into this curriculum for PMs who have worked with AI systems.

**Concrete example:** Devin is given a GitHub issue: "The login form doesn't validate email format." Devin reads the codebase, identifies the form component, writes a regex validator, adds unit tests, fixes the failing test case, and opens a pull request — without human intervention between the initial prompt and the PR.

---

## Part 11: Figma-Specific Terms

### 46. Dev Mode

**What it is:** Dev Mode is Figma's interface designed specifically for developers (as opposed to designers). It provides a code-oriented view of a Figma design file: developers can inspect exact measurements, colors, and spacing; view generated CSS and platform-specific code snippets; see component variants and their properties; and navigate to linked code components via Code Connect. Dev Mode is a separate access mode (with its own pricing tier) activated per file.

**Why it exists:** Designers and developers use Figma differently. Designers manipulate the canvas. Developers need to extract implementation specs — exact pixel values, spacing, color tokens, and the code that implements each component. Dev Mode creates a workspace optimized for that extraction workflow without cluttering the designer's view.

**Analogy:** Dev Mode is like a read-optimized interface for a data system — designers write to the design system (source of truth), developers read from it (implementation specs). The analogy holds for API documentation: Dev Mode is Figma's built-in API reference for every component in a design system.

**Where you'll see it:** Dev Mode is where developers interact with Figma designs during implementation. Figma Make builds on Dev Mode — Make's agent accesses the same component structure that Dev Mode exposes, but uses it to generate code rather than just display specs.

**Concrete example:** A developer opens a Figma file in Dev Mode to implement a new card component. They click the card, see `border-radius: 8px`, `padding: 16px 20px`, `box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)` — the exact CSS. They copy the generated CSS snippet, paste it into their stylesheet, and match the design exactly without back-and-forth with the designer.

---

### 47. Code Connect

**What it is:** Code Connect is a Figma feature that links Figma components to their code counterparts. Developers write a configuration file (a `.figma.tsx` or `.figma.ts` file) that declares "this Figma component maps to this React component, and these Figma properties map to these React props." Once connected, Dev Mode shows the actual code usage (e.g., `<Button variant="primary" size="lg">`) rather than generic CSS.

**Why it exists:** Before Code Connect, Dev Mode showed generated CSS approximations. A designer's `Button/Primary/Large` in Figma would show generic CSS — not the React component the engineering team actually uses. Code Connect bridges the gap: when a developer clicks that Figma component, they see the real `import { Button }` code with the correct props, copied directly from the codebase.

**Analogy:** Code Connect is like a binding between a design system's documentation and its code library — similar to linking a knowledge base entry to its implementation artifact. It makes the design-to-code traceability explicit and queryable.

**Where you'll see it:** Code Connect is a prerequisite for Figma Make to generate high-quality code. When Make's agent generates code for a Figma design that uses Code Connect components, it can generate `<Button variant="primary">` instead of `<div style={{ backgroundColor: '#1A73E8', borderRadius: '4px' }}>`. The code output references your actual component library.

**Concrete example:** Your team builds a design system. A developer runs `figma connect publish` which reads your Code Connect config files and uploads the mappings to Figma. Now, when anyone in Dev Mode or using Make clicks your `Input/Search` component, they see the real import statement and usage pattern — not a CSS approximation.

---

### 48. Make Kits (React 18 + Vite + esm.sh + Guidelines Doc)

**What it is:** A Make kit is the standard project scaffold that Figma Make uses for code generation. It's a minimal React project configured with: React 18 (the UI framework), Vite (the build tool and dev server), `esm.sh` (a CDN that serves npm packages as ES Modules, enabling import from URLs rather than `node_modules`), and a guidelines document (`GUIDELINES.md`) that instructs the AI agent how to write code for this project. Make kits are intentionally minimal — no Next.js, no router, no complex build pipeline.

**Why they exist:** Make needs a predictable, minimal target environment for code generation. A simpler environment means fewer edge cases for the AI agent to navigate, faster iteration, and more predictable output. The `esm.sh` CDN is particularly important: it means the Make kit can import npm packages without running `npm install` — the browser fetches them on demand from the CDN as ES Modules.

**Analogy:** The guidelines document in a Make kit is like a system prompt for the agent — it constrains the agent's behavior to conventions appropriate for this environment. `esm.sh` replacing `npm install` is like using pre-installed Lambda layers instead of packaging dependencies into each deployment ZIP — the packages are available at runtime from a shared source rather than bundled per-project.

**Where you'll see it:** Make kits are the primary artifact discussed in the platform PM curriculum. Understanding what each component does (why Vite not Webpack, why esm.sh not npm, why no Next.js) is core curriculum.

**Concrete example:** A Make kit's `index.html` might contain: `<script type="module">import React from 'https://esm.sh/react@18'; ...</script>`. No build step needed to import React — the browser fetches it from `esm.sh` as an ES Module. Vite's dev server serves the local files; `esm.sh` serves the npm dependencies. The whole setup runs in a browser tab via WebContainers.

---

### 49. Canvas-Direct AI Agents / MCP / use_figma Tool

**What it is:** Canvas-Direct AI Agents in Figma Make are AI agents that have programmatic access to the Figma canvas — they can read the design file's structure, component tree, properties, and layout, and use that data to generate or modify code. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the protocol (developed by Anthropic) that enables AI models to use external tools through a standardized interface. The `use_figma` tool is a specific MCP tool that gives an AI agent read/write access to Figma's canvas — letting it query design tokens, component properties, and layer structure.

**Why they exist:** Before canvas-direct agents, AI coding tools required developers to describe their design in natural language ("make a blue button with rounded corners"). Canvas-direct agents read the design directly — they see that the button is `8px border-radius`, `#3B82F6 fill`, and maps to the `Button/Primary` component — and generate code from that structured data, not from an imprecise description.

**Analogy:** MCP is the standardized tool-use protocol that Claude Code uses for filesystem tools, search, and external integrations. The `use_figma` tool follows the same MCP interface contract. The difference is the tool's domain: instead of reading files or querying databases, `use_figma` reads Figma's canvas data model. Canvas-direct agents use tool use to close the loop between design intent and code output.

**Where you'll see it:** This is the core architecture of Figma Make. Platform PM candidates are expected to understand how agents use tools to access design data, how MCP enables the Figma-to-code pipeline, and what decisions were made in designing this tool interface.

**Concrete example:** A Make agent receives the prompt "implement the hero section from the designs." It calls `use_figma({frame: "Hero Section"})` and receives structured data: a 1440px-wide frame containing a heading (Inter 56px, bold, color token `text/primary`), a subheading, and a CTA button (linked to the `Button/Primary` Code Connect component). It generates React code using those exact values — not approximations.

---

### 50. Figma Widgets (The Architectural Predecessor to Code Layers)

**What it is:** Figma Widgets are interactive UI elements that live directly on the Figma canvas — they're not plugins (which open in a separate panel). A Widget looks like a Figma design layer but runs code: you can click buttons, fill forms, and see data update — all within the canvas. Widgets are written in React-like code using Figma's Widget API. They represent Figma's first major step toward making the canvas a live computing environment rather than a static design surface.

**Why they exist:** Plugins are powerful but disruptive — they open a separate panel, breaking the design flow. Widgets integrate interactive, data-driven elements directly into the design canvas — voting buttons in a design review, live data pulled from a spreadsheet, a color token picker embedded in a design file. They're canvas-native interactivity.

**Analogy:** Figma Widgets are like embedded live data views — interactive, data-aware elements embedded directly in the workspace rather than requiring navigation to a separate interface. The architectural distinction (embedded canvas-native vs external plugin panel) maps to the difference between inline tools in an agent loop vs. external tool calls that break the agent's flow.

**Where you'll see it:** The curriculum frames Widgets as the architectural predecessor to Code Layers (Figma Make's mechanism for embedding live, AI-generated code directly in the canvas). Widgets proved that live code could run in the canvas; Code Layers extends this by making the code AI-generated and design-synchronized.

**Concrete example:** A design team uses a Widget to embed a live color palette pulled from their brand guidelines API — the Widget makes an HTTP request, displays the current brand colors as swatches on the canvas, and lets designers click to apply them to selected layers. This interactivity lives in the canvas, not in a separate plugin panel. When a brand color changes at the API, the Widget updates on the canvas automatically.

---

*End of Primer — 50 terms across 11 domains. Return to any section as needed during curriculum study.*
